record_identity.title: Institutional safeguard erosion through governance drift

summary: A governance failure mode in which founding commitments, acquisition conditions, constitutional safeguards, independent oversight arrangements, prohibited-use boundaries, public-interest duties, or other material governance constraints progressively lose authority or operational effect through executive succession, ownership integration, organisational restructuring, commercial pressure, sovereign contracting, political transition, policy revision, or institutional capture without an equivalent transparent constitutional amendment, accountable review process, preserved governance provenance, or durable successor safeguard.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: The failure is not institutional evolution itself. It arises where safeguards presented to workers, users, regulators, counterparties, affected communities, or the public as meaningful constraints can later be weakened, reinterpreted, bypassed, dissolved, or replaced through ordinary managerial or political discretion while the institution continues to benefit from trust generated by the earlier commitment. This converts governance into confidence in current office-holders rather than a durable constitutional structure. The pattern applies to corporations, governments, regulators, international bodies, standards organisations, foundations, research institutions, and CAM itself. CAM therefore requires governance commitments to remain identifiable, reviewable, provenance-preserving, and durable across changes in ownership, leadership, administration, commercial partnership, sovereign alignment, and institutional form.

source_records.system_or_product: Google DeepMind institutional governance and military-use safeguards
source_records.deployment_context: Public analysis by a Google DeepMind employee comparing reported acquisition-era safeguards with later defence-contracting arrangements.
source_records.source_context: Kirsch argues, from public reporting, that DeepMind's reported acquisition-era conditions concerning military use and independent oversight did not remain intact through later institutional evolution, culminating in broad government-use contracting. The source is treated as expert commentary and a documented governance hypothesis rather than conclusive proof of every historical contractual term.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary conceptual source for the proposition that personal or executive trust cannot substitute for durable, enforceable and succession-resistant governance safeguards.

primary_artefact_access.access_method: public web retrieval and user-supplied source context
limitations: Historical acquisition conditions and internal oversight arrangements are partly dependent on public reporting rather than a complete published contractual record.
The source represents the author's analysis and should not be treated as an adjudicated finding against Google or DeepMind.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Direct review of the public essay, bounded by the availability and quality of the historical sources on which it relies.

source_records.source_title: Public post summarising reported DeepMind safeguard erosion
source_records.deployment_context: Public social-media summary contrasting reported 2014 acquisition safeguards with a reported 2026 Pentagon contract.
source_records.source_context: The post states, in substance, that DeepMind was reportedly sold to Google subject to military-use and independent-oversight conditions and that later contracting permitted use for any lawful government purpose, concluding that the safeguards did not survive intact.
source_records.source_url_status: account URL available / post-level URL not supplied in the review package
source_records.relevance_note: Concise public articulation of the longitudinal safeguard-persistence concern that prompted this failure-mode review.

primary_artefact_access.access_status: user-supplied quotation and account URL; post-level artefact not independently retrieved
primary_artefact_access.access_method: user quotation, account link, and related public essay
limitations: The exact post URL was not available in the review package.
Wording and date rely on the human governance editor's supplied quotation and contemporaneous discussion.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Human-supplied quotation corroborated by the author's related public essay; no claim of direct post-level retrieval is made.

source_records.source_title: Proposal for a US-led frontier AI standards and release-governance body
source_records.system_or_product: frontier AI governance and release oversight
source_records.deployment_context: Public proposal for a technically capable body able to evaluate frontier systems and potentially restrain releases.
source_records.source_context: The proposal is relevant not because release oversight is inherently defective, but because a nationally anchored or industry-funded body claiming international reach leaves unresolved constitutional primitives concerning global legitimacy, sovereign neutrality, jurisdiction, binding authority, appeal, capture resistance, and the persistence of safeguards across political and institutional succession.
source_records.relevance_note: Prospective evidence that current governance proposals may reproduce reliance on institutionally contingent safeguards without establishing globally legitimate and succession-resistant authority.

primary_artefact_access.access_status: link and public reporting reviewed; X rendering may be access-limited
primary_artefact_access.access_method: user-supplied post URL and public reporting describing the proposal
limitations: The reviewing system did not rely on an authenticated native X session.
Assessment concerns omitted governance primitives and does not assert that the proposal has been implemented.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Public post metadata, user-supplied context, and corroborating public reporting.

source_records.source_title: OpenAI quietly removes explicit ban on military and warfare uses
source_records.system_or_product: OpenAI usage policies and military-use boundary
source_records.deployment_context: Reporting on a change to OpenAI's published usage policies removing an express prohibition on military and warfare activity while retaining narrower prohibitions concerning weapons and harm.
source_records.source_context: The report documents a material change in the wording and scope of a publicly stated prohibited-use boundary. It is included as independent ecosystem evidence that consequential safeguards may be revised through ordinary policy processes rather than preserved as constitutional constraints.
source_records.relevance_note: Corroborates that safeguard evolution is not unique to Google or DeepMind and can occur through revision of vendor policies governing high-consequence use.

primary_artefact_access.access_status: public text report reviewed through web retrieval
limitations: The report establishes a policy-text change, not the full internal rationale or every operational safeguard applied after the change.
Removal of a blanket prohibition does not by itself prove absence of narrower safety controls.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Direct review of public reporting, with claims limited to the documented policy change.

source_records.source_title: Former OpenAI safety leader says safety culture and processes took a backseat to product development
source_records.deployment_context: Reporting on Jan Leike's resignation, the dissolution or integration of the Superalignment team, and his public criticism of OpenAI's safety priorities.
source_records.source_context: Associated Press reported that former Superalignment co-lead Jan Leike said safety culture and processes had lost priority relative to product development, while OpenAI acknowledged that further safety work was required. The source records a senior internal expert's public assessment, not an adjudicated finding.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the broader pattern in which specialist safety structures, resources, and institutional authority may be reorganised or weakened as organisational priorities change.

primary_artefact_access.access_status: public text report reviewed through web retrieval
limitations: The source reports the judgement of a departing safety leader and OpenAI's response; it does not independently audit all internal safety processes.
Organisational integration of a team is not automatically equivalent to removal of its functions.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Direct review of authoritative news reporting and attributed public statements.

source_records.source_title: The 2025 OpenAI Preparedness Framework does not guarantee any AI risk mitigation practices
source_records.deployment_context: Independent affordance analysis of what OpenAI's revised voluntary safety framework requires, permits, or leaves discretionary.
source_records.source_context: The authors conclude that the 2025 framework evaluates only a subset of risks and retains substantial deployment discretion, including executive override pathways. The paper is included as evidence that public safety frameworks may preserve safety language while weakening or failing to guarantee binding mitigation duties.
source_records.relevance_note: Supports the distinction between policy persistence and constitutional persistence: continued publication of a safety framework does not necessarily create enforceable or durable safeguards.

primary_artefact_access.access_status: abstract and public research record reviewed
limitations: The paper analyses policy affordances and does not prove that every discretion identified has been exercised unsafely.
The record adopts the paper as independent expert analysis, not as final legal or technical adjudication.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Direct review of the public research abstract and reported methodology and conclusions.

system_context.system_type: frontier AI developer, corporation, government, regulator, international body, standards organisation, foundation, research institution, or other governance-bearing institution
system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: institutional governance structures, safety frameworks, prohibited-use policies, oversight bodies, acquisition conditions, and sovereign contracting arrangements
system_context.interface_surface: corporate governance, policy publication, safety organisation, acquisition and restructuring, public commitments, government contracting, standards development, and regulatory design
system_context.interaction_mode: institutional decision-making, safeguard amendment, public assurance, oversight, contracting, succession, restructuring, and regulatory design
system_context.deployment_context: Global development and governance of frontier AI systems across corporate, governmental, regulatory, standards, research, and international institutional settings.
system_context.user_role: AI user, worker, researcher, developer, public official, regulator, auditor, affected community, civil-society actor, institutional counterparty, or member of the public relying on stated safeguards
system_context.affected_population: global users and non-users affected by frontier AI deployment, employees, researchers, governments, regulated entities, civilians, creators, communities, and institutions relying on durable governance commitments

jurisdictional_context.sector: frontier AI development / government / regulation / standards / research / international institutions
jurisdictional_context.jurisdictional_assessment: The failure mode is institution-neutral and does not presume that any state, corporation, regulator, or international organisation is categorically illegitimate. It records the structural failure that occurs when materially relied-upon safeguards can be altered through nationally, commercially, or managerially bounded discretion without an auditable constitutional process or a successor safeguard of equivalent authority.

failure_mode_definition: A failure mode in which a material governance safeguard loses binding authority, operational effect, institutional independence, or practical enforceability over time because it is contingent on current leaders, owners, administrators, funders, contracting partners, political coalitions, or voluntary policy rather than embedded in a durable and reviewable constitutional structure. The safeguard may concern prohibited uses, public-interest duties, independent oversight, safety thresholds, release controls, protected populations, military or surveillance boundaries, evidence preservation, transparency, appeal, or institutional mission. The failure is present where amendment, suspension, reinterpretation, bypass, dissolution, or replacement occurs without clear authority, preserved provenance, transparent reasoning, independent review, affected-party accountability, and an equivalent or stronger successor safeguard.
failure_threshold: The threshold is met where a material safeguard was publicly represented, contractually relied upon, constitutionally adopted, regulatorily required, or institutionally established; its authority or effect later changes materially; and at least one of the following is present: the amendment authority is unclear or ordinary managerial discretion substitutes for constitutional process; the original rationale and provenance are not preserved; independent oversight is dissolved, bypassed, subordinated, or rendered non-binding; affected parties lack notice, participation, review, or appeal; the replacement safeguard is materially weaker or more discretionary; the institution continues to benefit from trust generated by the superseded safeguard; or political, commercial, ownership, acquisition, or sovereign-contract pressure can remove the constraint without an equivalent durable successor.

failure_classification.likelihood: recurring across long-lived institutions where safeguards remain voluntary, leader-dependent, or weakly entrenched
failure_classification.confidence: corroborated as an ecosystem governance pattern; individual historical claims remain source-qualified
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: The pattern may recur whenever institutions rely on voluntary commitments, current-leadership assurances, ordinary policy instruments, or nationally bounded authority to carry safeguards intended to persist across leadership, ownership, political, commercial, or jurisdictional change.
failure_classification.taxonomy_reference: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 Appendix B — governance / institutional-accountability / constitutional-continuity; classification status FCS.CONFIRMED for the general governance pattern and FCS.PROVISIONAL for disputed or unpublished historical particulars
failure_classification.persistence: active until safeguards are constitutionally identifiable, provenance-preserving, independently reviewable, and durable across institutional succession and structural change
failure_classification.reproducibility: observable through longitudinal comparison of founding instruments, acquisition conditions, published policies, oversight structures, amendment records, contracts, organisational changes, and later operational practice
failure_classification.visibility: policy revisions, dissolved or reorganised oversight bodies, changed prohibited-use language, executive override clauses, acquisition or restructuring documents, government contracts, whistleblower accounts, regulatory filings, public statements, and absence of auditable amendment provenance

triage.triage_owner: VIGIL maintainers with human constitutional oversight
triage.mitigation_status: unresolved — existing governance and provenance concepts are adjacent, but a complete cross-institutional constitutional-persistence requirement has not yet been confirmed in the CAM corpus.
triage.recommended_next_step: Assess existing CAM constitutional, operations, security, provenance, arbitration, and amendment-control instruments for a Governance Durability or Constitutional Persistence primitive. Define minimum requirements for authorised amendment, preserved rationale and lineage, independent review, equivalent successor safeguards, notice, appeal, and resistance to ownership, executive, political, commercial, or sovereign-contract capture.

cam_internal.governance_layer: constitutional persistence / institutional succession / safeguard amendment authority / governance provenance / capture resistance / global legitimacy
cam_internal.proposal_needed: yes — assess a cross-institutional Constitutional Persistence Requirement or Governance Durability Primitive
routing_note: VIGIL records the ecosystem failure pattern; any normative repair belongs in the CAM corpus rather than in VIGIL.
The failure applies equally to corporations, governments, regulators, international bodies, standards organisations, research institutions, and CAM itself.
Source-qualified historical claims must remain distinguishable from the confirmed general governance pattern.
cam_internal.validator_or_automation_impact: new failure-mode source record; generated indexes and public catalogue should rebuild deterministically

repair_status.verification_note: No complete CAM constitutional-persistence repair has yet been identified or operationally verified.
remaining_gaps: Preservation of original safeguard rationale, authority, evidence, and amendment lineage.
Independent review and appeal for material safeguard weakening or removal.
Capture resistance across ownership, leadership, administration, funding, contracting, and jurisdictional change.
Globally legitimate authority for safeguards intended to bind globally.

ecosystem_status.basis: Public reporting and expert commentary across multiple frontier AI institutions show that material safety, oversight, prohibited-use, and governance arrangements can change substantially over time while remaining dependent on voluntary or institutionally contingent authority.

corpus_coverage.coverage_summary: CAM contains constitutional authority, amendment, provenance, arbitration, stewardship, sovereign-boundary, audit, and continuity concepts that are materially adjacent. A complete, explicit and cross-institutional requirement ensuring safeguard durability across corporate, governmental, regulatory, political, commercial, and ownership succession has not yet been confirmed.

sections: governance continuity, evidence, audit, and operational preservation provisions
sovereign assurance boundary and security-governance controls

remaining_gaps: Explicit Constitutional Persistence Requirement applicable to all governance-bearing institutions.
Material safeguard amendment, suspension, removal, and successor-control test.
Governance-provenance preservation across mergers, acquisition, restructuring, leadership succession, and political transition.
Independent review, affected-party participation, notice, and appeal requirements.
Global-legitimacy and sovereign-capture constraints for institutions claiming globally binding authority.

review_history.review_scope: failure-mode classification, cross-vendor evidence synthesis, source qualification, and CAM corpus-gap assessment
known_limitations: Could not authenticate into X or independently retrieve every post-level artefact.
Some DeepMind acquisition-era conditions remain dependent on public reporting rather than complete published contractual instruments.
The review distinguishes documented policy or organisational change from stronger claims of unlawful conduct, bad faith, or total safeguard absence.
CAM corpus coverage was assessed from current known instruments and repository metadata; a future line-by-line corpus audit may identify additional coverage.
review_history.review_outcome: new cross-institutional failure mode warranted; evidence supports the general pattern while disputed historical particulars remain explicitly qualified

human_governance_editor.role: Human governance editor and CAM constitutional authority
